There's no one-size-fits-all treatment for PCOS. Your treatment plan will depend on your symptoms, health goals, and medical history. Here are some common options:

Birth control pills: Can regulate menstrual cycles and reduce acne.
Metformin: Helps with insulin resistance, which can improve ovulation and menstrual regularity.
Ovulation-inducing medications: Can help women with PCOS get pregnant.
Surgery: In some cases, ovarian surgery may be considered if medications aren't effective.

Each month, your body goes through changes to get ready for a possible pregnancy. This is called your menstrual cycle. Even if you don't want to get pregnant right now, your body still goes through these changes. Here's what happens, step by step:

Menstrual period (Days 1-7): The lining inside your uterus sheds and comes out as your period.
Follicular phase (Days 1-12): Eggs start developing in your ovaries, and the lining inside your uterus gets thicker.
Ovulation (Day 13): An egg is released from one of your ovaries.
Luteal phase (Days 14-28): If the egg isn't fertilized, the thick lining in your uterus breaks down, leading to the next menstrual period.
